author: @pacogil65
text: Dear Robert;I studied fabric design and I believe that heated fabrics can be easily made using a continuous thread made with a core of this conductive grapheme material. (Imagine Mylar but made with this stuff). Woven or knitted, breathable fabrics could be easily constructed using this material since the weft (or "fill"  as it is known in the US) in both types of fabric can be made using a continuous thread... I would like to know what Lurex, or Metlon Corporation think about this idea... - PS: Robert , you are a brilliant and honest man and an endless source of inspiration. Thanks for sharing!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: give it a go making some mate - no better way to demonstrate an idea than giving it a go - cheers

author: @sveindanielsolvenus
text:
Great stuff Robert :D

In the video you say the graphene concentrate is 5g pr liter (at 1:17). Then later in it you say it is a 5% graphene solution (at ca 2:30).
But a 5% solution in one liter, wouldn't that be 50 grams (or at least 50ml)? Am I thinking wrong?
5 divided by 100 is 0.05, which equals 5%.
5 divided by 1000 (1 liter), is 0.005, 0.5%.
I am sorry for my confusion. Maybe someone would like to clarify this for me?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +sveindaniel my bad it's 0.5%

replies.author: @sveindanielsolvenus
replies.text:
Thanks! That helps :)
Wow, just 0.5% of graphene has such effects, that is amazing stuff.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +sveindaniel yes it is mate lol

author: @Broseficus
text:
I know I'm late to this party, but after watching this video it came to mind that this rubberized coating process could be applied to fishing lines and then coiled up in a spiral to make an artificial muscle. The muscle would work by running an electrical current through the rubberized graphene paint rapidly increasing the temperature and causing contraction. Here is the link to the fishing line artificial muscle construction https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Tba8Nf02OSI

I think your rubberized graphene treatment can leap frog the development of artificial muscles. Imagine how many people you can help through prosthetics and robotics!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +bro sef good idea - cheers mate

author: @ET2uscg
text:
Hey Mr. Murray-Smith, I apologize if this is not the video that I should be posting on, but I had to let you know of what happened when I was making the graphene in the blender.  First I blended the graphite with water and liquid soap like you said and for only a bout 10 sec at a time, when ever I passed the blender I would push the button to start it and run it until my wife ask me to stop, which was around 10 secs or so.  I did this for about a week and then check the resistance of the dry powdered graphene at the top of the blender.  I got @ 59 ohms per inch.  I also tried to make some ink using the graphene and Future Floor wax the 100% acrylic.  after a week, letting it sit in a bowl and evaporating I scraped some out and it had become like a putty.  I check the resistance and it was around 4meg ohm per inch,  not good but I embeded a piece of magnesium in the putty and touched the positive probe to the putty and the neg probe to the magnesium ,  I read 1.7vdc.
I would like to know what I could do with the conductive putty, or is it just wasted?  Thanks Ed

author: @thegaryjay_yt
text:
I'm interested in applying something like this to fiberglass panels to add strength and/or to test how well it blocks extreme heats (which I will test on my own with your Indiegogo rewards). I was wondering if the ink/paint would be sprayable in something like a Preval sprayer system or even an industrial paint gun. If you have any feedback from your tests outside of what you've posted in videos, I'd very much appreciate it.

As soon as I figure out which of your products best fits my experimenting needs, I'll be investing in your campaign. Thank you very much for posting what you do; it's fascinating.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - there is no problem spraying the ink - it needs viscosity modifying - but to be honest that just means adding water

author: @thagrit
text: Hey Robert. Did you try this as a heating element yet? Heated cloths may be had more relevant soon since we may be headed into an extended minimum like the Maunder minimum. I was thinking that it would make a nice heated seat cover for a car if it will warm on 12 volts. I have no doubt that if it works well for heating that it could be used on a low voltage system. If for some reason not we can always use an inverter.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good points - i haven't tried it yet - but i will

author: @sasjadv
text:
Heated clothes sound like a great idea especially in November when winter in coming, but there is one thing I'm not sure about: our skin likes materials that can breathe or ventilate (like cotton) waterproof rubber sounds like the opposite of that.
Obvious solutions would be to make holes in the ink, to wear a cotton shirt under it or similar things...
But there is something called casein yarn, it can already breathe so why don't you give it a try? I think adding graphene into it should not be a problem

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest i think if i get anything to work i'll be happy - after that we can worry about niceties

replies.author: @sasjadv
replies.text:
Leonard Janus
As far as I know allergic people can not eat/drink the casein. And in milk the lactose is the thing that causes most allergenic reactions, not the casein.
I could be wrong about this, I haven't checked it.
Robert Murray-Smith I think casein yarn can be as exiting as rubber.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
sasja de vries
I agree mate and with your comments about allergies - but people worry about the strangest of things so mainly - I let them worry - even when I think it is nothing to worry about
